summary: A Shi'a Persian empire that ruled Iran from 1501 to 1736, one of the three early modern "gunpowder empires" alongside the Mughals and Ottomans. Founded by Ismail I with the military backing of the Qizilbash ("red-head") Turkoman warriors after a decisive victory at Sharur over the Aq Qoyunlu, it forcibly converted Persia to Twelver Shi'ism, suffered a shattering defeat to the Ottomans' Selim the Grim at the 1514 Battle of Chaldiran, and reached its apex under Shah Abbas the Great, who curbed the Qizilbash with a new ghulam slave army (organized with the help of English advisors Robert and Anthony Shirley), moved the capital to Isfahan, and deported Armenian merchants to New Julfa. It was chronicled by European travelers like Jean Chardin and Adam Olearius before its collapse and formal end under the Afsharid conqueror Nadir Shah in 1736.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The empire is identified in giveaways as the Shi'a Persian/Iranian dynasty that ruled from the 16th to 18th centuries (1501-1736), founded by Ismail I and reaching its peak under Shah Abbas the Great, with a capital ultimately at Isfahan. It is regularly grouped with the Mughal and Ottoman Empires as one of the three early modern "gunpowder empires," a term coined by historian Marshall Hodgson.

clues.clue: Ismail I founded the empire; Shah Abbas the Great was its most celebrated ruler
examples: For 10 points, name this empire founded by Shah Ismail that ruled Persia between the 16th and 18th centuries.
For 10 points, Shah Abbas the Great ruled over what 16th to 18th century Iranian gunpowder empire?

clues.clue: Grouped with the Mughal and Ottoman Empires as one of the three "gunpowder empires" (a term coined by historian Marshall Hodgson)
examples: For 10 points, the three early modern Gunpowder Empires were the Mughals, the Ottomans, and what Iranian empire?
Marshall Hodgson coined the term "gunpowder empire" to describe the Mughals, Ottomans, and this empire.

clues.clue: Established Twelver Shi'a Islam as Iran's state religion
examples: name this Persian dynasty that ruled from 1501 to 1736, which saw the establishment of Shia Islam as the state religion.
For 10 points, name this empire founded by Ismail I, who made Shia Islam the state religion in Iran.

works.name: Founding: Ismail I, the Qizilbash & the Conquest of Persia (c.1500-1514)
works.description: The dynasty traced its origins to a Sufi order based in the town of Ardabil, militarized by Sheikh Junayd and then Sheikh Haydar, whose followers wore a distinctive twelve-gored crimson hat ("Haydar's Crown") symbolizing the Twelve Shia Imams and became known as the Qizilbash ("red heads"); Haydar was killed fighting the rival White Sheep (Aq Qoyunlu) Turkomans, and his mausoleum in Ardabil became a pilgrimage site. His descendant Ismail I proclaimed the empire after a decisive victory over the Aq Qoyunlu at Sharur, rallied Qizilbash Turkoman forces to seize Tabriz as his capital, and defeated the Uzbek leader Muhammad Shaybani at the Battle of Merv, having his skull turned into a jeweled drinking goblet.

clues.clue: Traced its origins to a Sufi order in Ardabil, militarized first by Sheikh Junayd and then Sheikh Haydar, who was killed fighting the White Sheep (Aq Qoyunlu) Turkomans of Uzun Hasan and whose Ardabil mausoleum became a pilgrimage site
examples: Sheikh Haydar militarized the Sufi order that developed into this empire, which was founded in the city of Ardabil.
This group's founder Sheikh Haydar was killed fighting the White Sheep Turkomans, after which his mausoleum in Ardabil became a pilgrimage site.

clues.clue: The Qizilbash ("red heads") took their name from a twelve-gored crimson hat, sometimes called "Haydar's Crown," symbolizing the Twelve Shia Imams
examples: A distinctive twelve-gored crimson hat lent its name to a religious movement whose members formed the early core of this empire's army, the Qizilbash.
Name this force which took their name from their distinctive headwear, which was sometimes called Haydar's Crown.

clues.clue: Ismail I proclaimed the empire after a decisive victory over the Aq Qoyunlu ("White Sheep") at the Battle of Sharur, having taken their capital
examples: This empire was proclaimed following a decisive victory at Sharur over Aq Qoyunlu.
The White Sheep lost their capital to the founder of this dynasty, who later took Khorasan from the Uzbeks and was called Ismail I.

clues.clue: Ismail I rallied Turkoman Qizilbash forces to take Tabriz, which he made his capital
examples: This dynasty, which traced its descent to a Sufi Kurdish mystic in Ardabil, rallied the Turkoman Qizilbash to take the city of Tabriz.
He led many Qizilbash forces in the taking of Tabriz, which he set up as capital of the Safavid Empire.

clues.clue: Ismail I defeated the Uzbek leader Muhammad Shaybani at the Battle of Merv and had his skull turned into a jeweled drinking goblet
examples: The first ruler of this empire created a bejeweled goblet from the skull of Muhammad Shaybani, whom he defeated at the Battle of Merv.
That ruler of this dynasty ordered a rival's skull to be made into a jeweled drinking goblet after Muhammad Shaybani was killed in the Battle of Merv.

clues.clue: Ismail I wrote poetry in Turkic (Azerbaijani) under the pen name "Khata'i," including the Dahnama, and named his children after Shahnameh characters
examples: What language's poetry was pioneered by a ruler who named his progeny after Shahnameh characters? That ruler used the pen name "Khata'i" to write the Dahnama in a predecessor to this modern Turkic language.

works.name: Battle of Chaldiran & the Ottoman-Safavid Wars
works.description: In 1514, Ottoman Sultan Selim the Grim (Selim I) crushed the empire's Qizilbash cavalry with Janissary musket fire at the Battle of Chaldiran, the climax of Selim's retaliation for Safavid support of Shahkulu's Alevi rebellion; Ismail I himself was wounded, and the defeat "shattered" him and began the splintering of the Qizilbash confederation. The war ended with the Peace (Treaty) of Amasya, which split Armenia and Georgia between the two powers; the rivalry recurred in later conflicts including the week-long, torch-lit "Battle of the Torches" and a joint siege of Van, and was eventually consolidated by the Treaty of Zuhab (Qasr-e Shirin), which extended Amasya's terms in the Ottomans' favor. Decades later, Shah Abbas the Great turned the tide, defeating the Ottomans in 1618 and reconquering the Caucasus, Western Iran, Dagestan, and Mesopotamia.

clues.clue: In 1514, Ottoman Sultan Selim the Grim (Selim I) defeated the empire's Qizilbash cavalry (decimated by Janissary musket fire) at the Battle of Chaldiran, wounding/"shattering" Ismail I and halting the empire's western expansion
examples: The Peace of Amasya ended a war that saw a battle in which this empire's Qizilbash cavalry was decimated by musket fire from Selim the Grim's Janissaries.
That army of one of these powers was composed of the Qizilbash, but was defeated by the other side at the Battle of Chaldiran by forces led by Selim the Grim, a loss that shattered Ismail I.

clues.clue: Chaldiran was the climax of Selim I's retaliatory 1514 campaign against Safavid support for Shahkulu's Alevi rebellion; the Qizilbash confederation began to splinter after the defeat and the sack of Tabriz
examples: This 1514 battle was the climax of Selim I's retaliatory campaign against Safavid support for Sahkulu's rebellion. The Qizilbash confederation began to splinter after Selim won this battle and pillaged Tabriz.

clues.clue: The war was ended by the Peace (Treaty) of Amasya, which forced the empire to forfeit territory and split Armenia and Georgia between the Safavids and Ottomans
examples: This empire forfeited territory in the peace of Amasya, and the power of its Qizilbash was diminished after its defeat at the Battle of Chaldiran by the Ottoman Empire.
The Battle of Chaldiran began the Ottoman-Safavid War, which was ended by this treaty. Armenia and Georgia were split between the two by this peace.

clues.clue: A separate battle against the Ottomans, fought continuously day and night for a week, was called the "Battle of the Torches"; the two empires also jointly besieged Van, advised by the Frenchman Gabriel de Luetz
examples: A battle between these two powers continued both day and night for a week, giving it the appropriate name Battle of the Torches as both sides fought by torchlight. The Frenchman Gabriel de Luetz helped advise one of these powers during its siege of the other's city of Van.

clues.clue: The Treaty of Zuhab (Qasr-e Shirin) consolidated and extended the Peace of Amasya in the Ottomans' favor, ending a later war
examples: These two powers signed the Treaty of Zuhab with each other, which was a consolidation of the earlier Peace of Amasya signed between them.
The Treaty of Zuhab, or Qasr-e Shirin, ended this dynasty's war against its neighbor, who had defeated them at Chaldiran.

clues.clue: Shah Abbas the Great defeated the Ottomans in 1618, reconquering the Caucasus, Western Iran, Dagestan, and Mesopotamia with Janissary-inspired ghulam infantry
examples: Name this leader who defeated the Ottoman Empire in 1618, allowing Persia to retake the Caucasus and Western Iran.
This empire's ruler Abbas I reconquered Caucasus territories with Janissary-inspired ghulam infantry.

clues.clue: The Ottoman rebel Prince Bayezid, son of Suleiman the Magnificent, fled to the Safavid court after starting a rebellion
examples: Prince Bayezid fled to the Safavid court after starting a rebellion in this empire.

works.name: The Qizilbash: Rise and Fall of the Red Heads
works.description: The Qizilbash — Turkoman "murid" cavalry drawn largely from tribes such as the Shamlu, Rumlu, and Ustajlu, wearing the distinctive twelve-gored red headgear — formed the empire's early military and administrative backbone and were motivated by "millenarian" verses proclaiming "I am Alexander" and "I am God's mystery," written under Ismail I's pen name Khata'i. Loyalty was tested with rituals like eating pieces of a rival's body off a skewer, and the cleric Ali al-Karaki forced them to abandon their oral traditions about Abu Muslim; their power was eventually curbed and their administrative dominance eclipsed when Shah Abbas the Great replaced them with a new ghulam slave army of Caucasian converts.

clues.clue: Drawn from Turkoman tribes such as the Shamlu, Rumlu, and Ustajlu, wearing twelve-gored red headgear; formed the empire's early military and administrative backbone, exemplifying a "millenarian collectivism" with verses proclaiming "I am Alexander" and "I am God's mystery"
examples: The most powerful subdivision within a movement associated with this empire that exemplified "millenarian collectivism" was the Shamlu tribe.
This group was motivated by poems proclaiming "I am Alexander" and "I am God's mystery" written under the pen-name Khata'i.

clues.clue: Loyalty was tested by forcing members to eat pieces of Murad Beg's body off a skewer; the cleric Ali al-Karaki forced them to abandon their oral tradition of Abu Muslim legends
examples: According to eyewitness accounts, members of this group were forced to prove their loyalty by eating pieces of Murad Beg's body off a skewer.
Ali al-Karaki forced this group to give up its oral tradition of legends about Abu Muslim.

clues.clue: Their power diminished after the split following Chaldiran and dissension between constituent tribes; Shah Abbas the Great ultimately curbed and purged them, replacing them in the army with Caucasian ghulam slave soldiers
examples: Its power diminished thanks to dissension between its constitutive tribes, such as the Ustajlu and Rumlu.
One of Abbas's first actions as shah was to curb the power of this group of warriors, replacing them in the army with ghulams.

clues.clue: Their administrative dominance was undermined by Georgian and Armenian slaves converted to Islam in the 17th century after Abbas decided to replace them with a ghulam army; bodyguards called qurchis assassinated a rival claimant amid the resulting power struggle
examples: Their administrative dominance was undermined by Georgian and Armenian slaves converted to Islam in the 17th century, after Shah Abbas decided to replace them with a ghulam army.
Bodyguards known as qurchis assassinated a claimant to this empire after his sister betrayed him for a brother who had been imprisoned in the Qahqaheh Castle.

works.name: Tahmasp I & the Succession Crisis
works.description: Tahmasp I, the son of Ismail I and the dynasty's longest-reigning shah, moved the capital to Qazvin, signed the Peace of Amasya with the Ottomans, sheltered the fugitive Ottoman Prince Bayezid, helped the Mughal emperor Humayun retake his throne, and ordered a massacre of the Takkalu tribe after an Uzbek invasion of Khorasan. After his death, a bloody succession crisis followed: his daughter Pari Khan Khanum poisoned her sister to help bring a nearly blind ruler to power before being strangled by the Qizilbash herself, while his son Ismail II tried to restore Sunni Islam before probably dying of poison.

clues.clue: Tahmasp I, son and successor of Ismail I, was the dynasty's longest-reigning shah; he moved the capital to Qazvin and grew devout later in life, shunning poets like Fuzuli
examples: The longest reigning Shah of this dynasty was the son of Ismail I and was Tahmasp I.
A holder of this title moved his capital to Qazvin and shunned poets like Fuzuli later in life as his faith deepened; that holder of this title was the devout Tahmasp I.

clues.clue: Tahmasp I signed the Peace of Amasya with the Ottomans and sheltered the fugitive Ottoman Prince Bayezid at his court
examples: This second shah of the Safavid empire and son of Ismail I signed the Peace of Amasya with the Ottoman empire.
This Safavid shah offered asylum to the wayward Prince Bayezid and helped Humayun retake the Mughal Empire.

clues.clue: Tahmasp I helped the Mughal emperor Humayun recapture the Mughal throne
examples: Humayun managed to recapture the Mughal empire with the help of Tahmasp I, a ruler of this empire and the son of its founder Ismail I.

clues.clue: Tahmasp I ordered a massacre of the Takkalu tribe after the Uzbeks invaded Khorasan
examples: He also ordered a massacre of the Takkalu tribe after the Uzbeks invaded Khorasan.

clues.clue: After Tahmasp I's death, his daughter Pari Khan Khanum poisoned her sister to bring a nearly blind ruler to power during the succession crisis, but was herself strangled by the Qizilbash
examples: In this empire, Pari Khan Khanum was strangled by the Qizilbash during the succession crises that arose following the death of her father Tahmasp I.
A nearly blind head of this empire came to power after Pari Khan Khanum poisoned her sister.

clues.clue: Tahmasp I's son Ismail II tried to restore Sunni Islam and probably died of poison
examples: This man's son, Ismail II, tried to restore Sunni Islam and probably died of poison.

works.name: Abbas the Great: Reform, Reconquest & the Move to Isfahan
works.description: Shah Abbas I ("the Great") came to power amid Qizilbash assassinations and coups, reasserted royal authority, and moved the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan in 1598. He built a new, permanent tripartite army (topchis, tofangchis, and ghulam slave soldiers) with the help of English military advisors Robert and Anthony Shirley to curb the Qizilbash, retook the port of Hormuz from the Portuguese with English East India Company help (founding the rival port of Bandar-e Abbas), captured Kandahar for the Mughal emperor Jahangir, and forcibly deported Armenian merchants and craftsmen to a new suburb, New Julfa, which became a hub of silk production.

clues.clue: Came to power amid Qizilbash assassinations and coups, then overcame their power to restore royal supremacy; moved the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan in 1598
examples: This dynasty's ruler Abbas I moved its capital from Qazvin to Isfahan.
'Abbas the Great moved the Safavid capital to this city in 1598.

clues.clue: Built a new, permanent army with a tripartite structure of topchis, tofangchis, and ghulam slave soldiers, organized with the help of English advisors Robert and Anthony Shirley
examples: This dynasty's greatest ruler established a permanent army with a tripartite structure of topchis, tofangchis, and ghulam slaves.
After his military was modernized by the Shirley brothers, he retook Tabriz from the Ottomans and captured Kandahar for Jahangir.

clues.clue: Retook the port of Hormuz from the Portuguese with help from the English East India Company, then founded the rival port of Bandar-e Abbas, leading to Hormuz's decline
examples: This dynasty captured Hormuz from the Portuguese and converted it into a trading port for the English East India Company.
A ruler of this dynasty founded an eponymous port, or Bandar, that led to the decline of Hormuz as a trading center.

clues.clue: Forcibly deported Armenian merchants and craftsmen (the "Great Surgun") to a new suburb of Isfahan, New Julfa, which became a hub of silk production and trade
examples: After this dynasty enacted the Great Surgun, New Julfa became a hub of silk production.
That ruler of this empire forcibly resettled Armenian craftsmen after moving the capital from Qazvin.

clues.clue: A former slave, Allahverdi Khan, became a military leader in the empire after helping assassinate a rival named Ostaglu
examples: A former slave named Allahverdi Khan became a military leader in this polity after helping assassinate Ostaglu.

clues.clue: Abbas the Great expanded the ghulam conscription system to reconquer provinces including Dagestan and Mesopotamia
examples: Name this ruler, who expanded the ghilman conscription system to reconquer several provinces, including Dagestan and Mesopotamia, that had been lost shortly before their rule.

clues.clue: Executed his own son after the son pre-empted the killing of a boar with a spear
examples: A monarch of this empire had his son executed for pre-empting the killing of a boar with a spear.

works.name: Isfahan: Architecture & Art
works.description: As capital, Isfahan was centered on the vast Naqsh-e Jahan Square, from which rulers watched public events in the Ali Qapu palace; Abbas the Great built the Chehel Sotoun palace and, after Tabriz was sacked, the Shah Mosque and the Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque on the square, the latter distinguished by an L-shaped entrance and carpet-like tilework listing the Twelve Shia Imams around its mihrab. The empire's most celebrated illustrated manuscript, the Houghton Shahnameh — featuring paintings such as "The Court of Gayumars" and a depiction of three haloed saints on the "Ship of Shiism" — was gifted by a Safavid ruler to Ottoman Sultan Selim II.

clues.clue: Isfahan's rulers resided in the Ali Qapu palace overlooking the vast Naqsh-e Jahan Square; Abbas the Great built the palace of Chehel Sotoun there
examples: Rulers of this empire resided in Ali Qapu palace, which allowed them to supervise public events occurring in Naqsh-e-Jahan Square.
One leader of this dynasty built the palace of Chehel Sotoun.

clues.clue: Built the Shah Mosque and the Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque on Naqsh-e Jahan Square after Tabriz was sacked; the latter has an L-shaped entrance and carpet-like tilework listing the Twelve Shia Imams around its mihrab
examples: This dynasty built the Shah and Shkeykh Loft'Allah mosques on the Naqsh-i-Jehan square of its capital after Tabriz was sacked.

Its capital has a mosque with an L-shaped entrance and a window and a yellow tile medallion above the mihrab. That mosque's tilework is notably carpetlike and contains a list of the twelve shia imams around the mihrab.

clues.clue: The Houghton Shahnameh, the empire's most famous illustrated book — with paintings including "The Court of Gayumars" and three haloed saints on the "Ship of Shiism" — was gifted by a Safavid ruler to Ottoman Sultan Selim II
examples: Perhaps the most famous book of this dynasty has a painting of three saints with flaming haloes on the "Ship of Shiism" while a painting showing three figures sitting among rocks overflow the borders of the composition, "The court of Gayumars". That book, the Houghton Shahnameh, was gifted to Selim II by a ruler of this dynasty.

clues.clue: Safavid artisans produced rugs/carpets named for the city of Ardabil; two large examples inscribed with poetry by Hafez are housed in London's Victoria and Albert Museum
examples: Safavid artisans made a variety of these things named for the city of Ardabil. Two large Ardabil examples of these things with an inscription by Hafez are housed in London's Victoria and Albert Museum.

works.name: European Travelers, Diplomats & Trade
works.description: The empire attracted numerous European travelers and diplomats, including the French jeweler Jean Chardin, whose Travels of Sir John Chardin recorded court life, coronation ceremonies, and the silk trade; the Spanish ambassador Garcia de Silva Figueroa, who conducted archaeological research at the ruins of Persepolis ("Chilminara"); Adam Olearius, who recorded a Holstein-Gottorp trade mission; and the English merchant Anthony Jenkinson. English brothers Robert and Anthony Shirley modernized the army; Robert married Teresa Sampsonia, a Circassian noblewoman later painted alongside him by Anthony Van Dyck. Administratively, the empire replaced hereditary soyurgal land grants with toyul grants for military men, employed shahbandar officials to tax and regulate port trade, and used toll-collecting rahdars to keep roads safe, impressing French travelers Thevenot and Tavernier.

clues.clue: French jeweler Jean Chardin's Travels of Sir John Chardin is an important source on the empire's court life, coronation ceremonies, and trade in materials like silk
examples: The Travels of Sir John Chardin is an important source on the court life of this polity, where Chardin was a jewel purchaser.
Jean Chardin recorded how the Safavid Empire traded four types of this material used to make carpets, such as the "shirvani" variety.

clues.clue: Spanish ambassador Garcia de Silva Figueroa, sent by Philip III, conducted archaeological research at the ruins of Persepolis ("Chilminara") and left a detailed travel account
examples: While serving as an ambassador, Don Garcia de Silva Figueroa conducted archaeological research in this empire.
This empire received the ambassador Garcia de Silva Figueroa, sent by Philip III of Spain, who left a detailed account of his travels and the ruins of "Chilminara."

clues.clue: Adam Olearius recorded two trade missions to the empire organized to stimulate trade with Friedrichstadt (a Holstein-Gottorp mission); Anthony Jenkinson also produced an account of visiting the royal court
examples: This empire was the more distant destination of the two journeys organised to stimulate trade with Friedrichstadt, recorded by Adam Olearius.
He wasn't Ivan the Terrible, but the English merchant Anthony Jenkinson produced an account of visiting this dynasty's royal court.

clues.clue: English brothers Robert and Anthony Shirley modernized the army; Robert married Teresa Sampsonia, a Circassian noblewoman later painted alongside him by Anthony Van Dyck
examples: Anthony Van Dyck painted portraits of Teresa Sampsonia and her husband Robert Shirley, who modernised this empire's military.
This Circassian noblewoman and "Ambassadress of the King of Persia" became a sensation in Jacobean England after marrying Robert Shirley during one of his embassies to the Safavid court.

clues.clue: Replaced hereditary soyurgal land grants with toyul grants for military men, employed shahbandar officials to tax/regulate port trade, and used toll-collecting rahdars to keep roads safe, impressing French travelers Thevenot and Tavernier
examples: The toyul developed under this dynasty after it abolished the hereditary soyurgals land grants and redistributed them to military men.
French travelers to this non-Indian dynasty like Thevenot and Tavernier were impressed by the safe roads governed by toll-taking rahdars.

works.name: Twelver Shi'ism: Conversion & Persecution
works.description: Ismail I forcibly converted Persia's population to Twelver Shi'ism, partly to forge a distinct identity against the Sunni Ottomans, creating the world's largest Twelver community; the tombs of Sunni figures Abu Hanifa and Abdul-Qadir Gilani were destroyed, and a failed provision of the Peace of Amasya would have ended the ritual cursing of the sahaba. The conversion campaign also drove Jewish persecution, chronicled by the poet Babai ben Lotf in his "Book of a Forced Convert," and pushed Mirwais Hotak to obtain a fatwa condemning it and found the rival Hotak Dynasty in Kandahar; the era also saw the philosopher Mulla Sadra and the Ishraqi School reach their height of influence.

clues.clue: Ismail I forcibly converted Persia's population to Twelver Shi'ism to forge a distinct identity against the Sunni Ottomans, creating the world's largest Twelver community
examples: This empire's population was forcibly converted to Twelver Shi'ism under its founder, Ismail I.
This process was begun largely to create a sense of identity for Isma'il I's empire in contrast to the Sunni Ottomans.

clues.clue: The tombs of Sunni figures Abu Hanifa and Abdul-Qadir Gilani were destroyed as part of the forced-conversion campaign; a failed Peace of Amasya provision would have ended the ritual cursing of the sahaba
examples: The tombs of Abu Hanifa and Abdul-Qadir Gilani were destroyed as part of this process.
A failed attempt to curb an extreme portion of this process was a never enacted term in the Treaty of Amasya requiring the cessation of the cursing of the sahaba.

clues.clue: Poet Babai ben Lotf wrote "Book of a Forced Convert," chronicling the persecution of the empire's Jews
examples: A poet in this empire named B'abai ben Lotf wrote a chronicle titled "Book of a Forced Convert" about the persecution of its Jews.

clues.clue: Mirwais Hotak obtained a fatwa on hajj condemning the forced-conversion policy and founded the rival Hotak Dynasty in Kandahar to escape it
examples: While in Mecca on the hajj, Mirwais Hotak obtained a fatwa decrying this process and founded the Hotak Dynasty in Kandahar to escape it.

clues.clue: The philosopher Mulla Sadra and the Ishraqi School reached the height of their influence during the dynasty
examples: During this dynasty, Mulla Sadra helped the Ishraqi School reach its height of influence.

clues.clue: A ruler's reign was chronicled in the "World-Adorning History" by the scribe Iskandar Beg Munshi, beginning with a coup by Murshid Qoli Khan
examples: A chronicle called the "World-Adorning History" of a ruler of this empire was written by the scribe Iskandar Beg Munshi.
The reign of a ruler of this dynasty was chronicled by Iskander Beg Munshi and began with a coup by Murshid Qoli Khan.

works.name: Decline & Fall
works.description: The empire declined in the early 18th century, culminating in the Hotak rebellion that deposed the last Safavid shah and led to the rise of the Afsharid dynasty from the Afshar clan; the conqueror Nadir Shah formally dismantled the dynasty in 1736. The Zand dynasty later succeeded the Safavids as rulers of Iran.

clues.clue: The Hotak rebellion deposed a Safavid shah, leading to the rise of the first ruling Turkoman dynasty from the Afshar clan
examples: The Hotak rebellion that deposed a holder of this title led to the rise of its first ruling Turkoman dynasty from the Afshar clan.

clues.clue: The Afsharid conqueror Nadir Shah dismantled/conquered the dynasty in 1736
examples: The Afsharid Nadir Shah conquered this dynasty in 1736.
Name this dynasty founded by Ismail I which unified modern Iran during the gunpowder age until it was dismantled by Nadir Shah.

clues.clue: The Zand dynasty succeeded the Safavids as rulers of Iran
examples: The Zand dynasty succeeded the Safavids in this nation.

comprehensive_summary: The Safavid Empire was a Shi'a Persian dynasty that ruled Iran from 1501 to 1736, founded by Ismail I and reaching its peak under Shah Abbas the Great; it is regularly grouped with the Mughal and Ottoman Empires as one of the three "gunpowder empires," a term coined by historian Marshall Hodgson, and it established Twelver Shi'a Islam as Iran's state religion. The dynasty traced its origins to a Sufi order in Ardabil, militarized first by Sheikh Junayd and then Sheikh Haydar (who was killed fighting the rival White Sheep, or Aq Qoyunlu, Turkomans, and whose Ardabil mausoleum became a pilgrimage site); his followers took the name Qizilbash ("red heads") from a twelve-gored crimson hat called "Haydar's Crown," symbolizing the Twelve Shia Imams. Ismail I proclaimed the empire after a decisive victory over the Aq Qoyunlu at the Battle of Sharur, rallied Turkoman Qizilbash forces to take Tabriz as his capital, and defeated the Uzbek leader Muhammad Shaybani at the Battle of Merv, turning his skull into a jeweled drinking goblet; Ismail also wrote poetry in Turkic under the pen name "Khata'i." In 1514, Ottoman Sultan Selim the Grim crushed the empire's Qizilbash cavalry with Janissary musket fire at the Battle of Chaldiran — the climax of Selim's retaliation for Safavid support of Shahkulu's Alevi rebellion — wounding and "shattering" Ismail I and beginning the splintering of the Qizilbash confederation; the resulting Peace of Amasya split Armenia and Georgia between the two empires, and their rivalry continued in conflicts like the torch-lit "Battle of the Torches" and a joint siege of Van (advised by the Frenchman Gabriel de Luetz) before the Treaty of Zuhab (Qasr-e Shirin) extended Amasya's terms in Ottoman favor. The Qizilbash — drawn from tribes like the Shamlu, Rumlu, and Ustajlu, and motivated by "millenarian" verses proclaiming "I am Alexander" and "I am God's mystery" — formed the empire's early military and administrative backbone, testing loyalty with rituals like eating a rival's flesh off a skewer, until the cleric Ali al-Karaki forced them to abandon their oral Abu Muslim traditions and their power was eventually curbed by Shah Abbas the Great. Tahmasp I, Ismail I's son and the dynasty's longest-reigning shah, moved the capital to Qazvin, signed the Peace of Amasya, sheltered the fugitive Ottoman Prince Bayezid, helped the Mughal emperor Humayun retake his throne, and massacred the Takkalu tribe after an Uzbek invasion of Khorasan; after his death, a succession crisis saw his daughter Pari Khan Khanum poison her sister to install a nearly blind ruler before being strangled by the Qizilbash herself, while his son Ismail II tried to restore Sunni Islam before probably dying of poison. Shah Abbas the Great came to power amid Qizilbash assassinations and coups, restored royal authority, and moved the capital from Qazvin to Isfahan in 1598, building a new tripartite army (topchis, tofangchis, and ghulam slave soldiers) with English advisors Robert and Anthony Shirley, retaking Hormuz from the Portuguese with English East India Company help and founding the rival port of Bandar-e Abbas, capturing Kandahar for the Mughal emperor Jahangir, reconquering the Caucasus and Mesopotamia from the Ottomans in 1618, and forcibly deporting Armenian merchants to the new suburb of New Julfa, which became a hub of silk production; a former slave, Allahverdi Khan, rose to military leadership after helping assassinate a rival. Isfahan's rulers watched public events from the Ali Qapu palace overlooking Naqsh-e Jahan Square, where Abbas built the Chehel Sotoun palace and, after Tabriz was sacked, the Shah Mosque and Sheikh Lotfollah Mosque (the latter noted for its L-shaped entrance and carpet-like tilework listing the Twelve Shia Imams); the empire's most celebrated illustrated manuscript, the Houghton Shahnameh, was gifted to Ottoman Sultan Selim II, and its artisans produced Ardabil carpets inscribed with poetry by Hafez, now in London's Victoria and Albert Museum. European travelers documented the empire extensively: the French jeweler Jean Chardin's Travels recorded court life and the silk trade, Spanish ambassador Garcia de Silva Figueroa conducted archaeological research at Persepolis, Adam Olearius chronicled Holstein-Gottorp trade missions, and Anthony Jenkinson described the royal court, while administratively the empire replaced hereditary soyurgal land grants with toyul grants, employed shahbandar port officials, and used toll-collecting rahdars to secure trade roads. Ismail I's forced conversion of Persia to Twelver Shi'ism, meant to forge an identity against the Sunni Ottomans, created the world's largest Twelver community but also drove the destruction of Sunni shrines, the persecution of the empire's Jews (chronicled by poet Babai ben Lotf), and Mirwais Hotak's flight to found the rival Hotak Dynasty in Kandahar, even as the philosopher Mulla Sadra and the Ishraqi School flourished and the scribe Iskandar Beg Munshi chronicled a ruler's reign in the "World-Adorning History." The empire finally succumbed to the Hotak rebellion, which deposed its last shah and paved the way for the Afsharid dynasty; the conqueror Nadir Shah formally dismantled the Safavids in 1736, after which the Zand dynasty succeeded them as rulers of Iran.
